title: Function Generator and Oscilloscope
description: Implementation of a function generator and a real-time oscilloscope and multimeter, built on two 8-bit PIC18F microcontrollers.
markdown:
## What
EasyScope is a two-part embedded instrumentation system built on dual 8-bit PIC18F microcontrollers running on EasyPIC V7 development boards. The first MCU acts as a low-frequency signal generator capable of producing DC, sinusoidal, triangular, and square waveforms with adjustable frequency (0–1000 Hz for sinusoidal and triangular, 1000 or 10 000 Hz for square) and configurable duty cycle (0.1 to 0.9) for the square signal. The second MCU implements an oscilloscope and multimeter that samples, stores, and displays incoming signals in real time on a GLCD screen.

## How
The signal generator uses a DAC to output waveforms computed in firmware, with frequency and duty cycle parameters driven by potentiometers and push buttons. 7-segment displays provide live parameter feedback to the user. The oscilloscope side reads analog input through an ADC at a 2000 Hz sampling rate, applies sub-sampling to fit the acquisition window into the constrained RAM, and renders the reconstructed waveform on the GLCD. All logic (waveform generation, ADC acquisition, display refresh, and user input handling) runs concurrently on 8-bit RISC cores with no operating system, requiring careful scheduling and memory management entirely in Assembly.

## Result
A fully functional benchtop instrument capable of generating and visualizing signals across the 0–10 000 Hz range. The system demonstrates stable real-time display of sinusoidal waveforms generated by the first MCU and captured by the second, validating the end-to-end signal chain. Key engineering constraints met: operating within a few kilobytes of RAM, performing all arithmetic in 8-bit, and sustaining real-time acquisition and rendering in parallel, all without a higher-level language or an RTOS.

### AI Usage (AI-Free)
None. The project was developed entirely in Assembly on constrained 8-bit hardware. All waveform math, ADC driver code, GLCD rendering routines, and inter-peripheral timing were implemented manually from datasheets and first principles, with no AI-assisted tooling involved.
